main_summary.summary: Euvester Simpson, interviewed in Jackson, Mississippi on March 11, 2013 by historian John Dittmer, recounts her path from the Mississippi Delta into SNCC and the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party. Born in Itta Bena and raised between Greenwood and a sharecropping plantation, she attended Amanda Elzy High School, then moved to Racine, Wisconsin at age 14 to live with her sister. Returning to Mississippi in January 1963, she attended a mass meeting in Greenwood where she met Hollis Watkins, Lawrence Guyot, John O'Neal, and her lifelong friend June Johnson. Recruited by Annelle Ponder for SCLC's Citizenship School in Charleston, South Carolina, she studied under Septima Clark, Myles Horton, and Esau Jenkins. On the return bus trip in June 1963, Simpson, Ponder, Fannie Lou Hamer, June Johnson, and others were arrested in Winona, Mississippi; she shared a cell with Hamer during the beatings and learned of Medgar Evers' assassination upon release. She worked the WATS line at the COFO office in Jackson during Freedom Summer 1964, attended the Atlantic City convention with the MFDP delegation, then enrolled at Tougaloo College. She later worked for the Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights Under Law, Friends of Children of Mississippi, and the Mid-South Delta Initiative.
key_themes: Mississippi Delta sharecropping and family land loss
Winona, Mississippi jailhouse beatings of June 1963
Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party and Atlantic City challenge
Tougaloo College and post-Movement community development
main_summary.historical_significance:
Simpson is one of the few surviving direct witnesses to the Winona jail beatings of Fannie Lou Hamer, Annelle Ponder, and June Johnson, events Hamer would describe at the 1964 Democratic National Convention. Her cell-by-cell account, including the gospel song 'Walk With Me' that the women sang to calm Hamer through the night, provides a first-person source on a foundational MFDP incident.

The interview also documents the institutional infrastructure of Freedom Summer from inside the Jackson COFO office: the WATS-line shift system that connected field workers to legal and medical support, the personnel mix of SNCC, CORE, SCLC, and NAACP staff who shared the space, and the post-Atlantic City rift between rural activists and those willing to accept the two-seat compromise. Simpson's later career, Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights, Friends of Children of Mississippi Head Start, the Kellogg Foundation's Mid-South Delta Initiative, and Hurricane Katrina case work, traces how Movement-era networks carried into Mississippi's community-development sector across five decades.

chapters.title: The Youngest of Seven in Itta Bena
chapters.topic: Birth and Family in the Delta
chapters.summary: Interviewed by John Dittmer in Jackson, Mississippi, in March 2013, Euvester Simpson opens on being born the youngest of seven children in Itta Bena, a butcher father who became a sharecropper, and a mother raised on her grandfather's farm in Mound Bayou, a great-grandfather who went to college after emancipation and became a teacher, minister, and landowner.

chapters.title: The Car That Would Not Crank
chapters.summary: Simpson tells how her mother met her father during a revival at her great-grandfather's church, asked God for a sign before eloping, took it as that sign when the brand-new Model T would not crank, ignored it, and married him the next night when the car started, after which the couple farmed rented land until both her father's friend and her great-grandfather lost their land and the family fell into sharecropping.

chapters.title: A School Built So Black Kids Could Pick Cotton
chapters.summary: She recounts her schooling at Ellis Rogers on the campus of Mississippi Valley State, the seventh-through-ninth grades in Itta Bena, the consolidated Amanda Elzy High School that bused Black children from across the county, and the split session that sent students to class in summer so they would be free to pick cotton in the fall, a system that helped push her parents to send her to Racine at fourteen.

chapters.part: The Most Southern Place on Earth
chapters.title: Everybody Knew Their Place
chapters.topic: Segregation and Daily Life in the Delta
chapters.summary: Simpson describes the totally segregated, economically depressed Delta of her childhood, a self-sufficient Black community of sharecroppers, day workers, teachers, preachers, and a few shop owners, families that had owned land until the 1920s or 1930s before losing it, and her own poor showing at chopping and picking cotton as a bug-fearing country girl.

chapters.part: The Most Southern Place on Earth
chapters.title: A Smokehouse Full of Hams
chapters.topic: Father the Butcher and the Migration North
chapters.summary: She remembers her butcher father killing neighbors' hogs in the fall for cuts of meat, the smokehouse hung with hams, her mother's homemade sausage and canned gardens, her much older siblings and an eighty-one-year-old brother in Detroit, and how the older boys, after working for the city of Greenwood, joined the Great Migration north to jobs in the auto industry.

chapters.part: The Most Southern Place on Earth
chapters.title: I Played the Rabbit in Winnie the Pooh
chapters.summary: She recounts her childhood passion to become an actor, landing leading roles in plays in Mississippi, and the cultural shock of Racine, where a school of roughly ten percent Black students never cast her in the dramatic club's productions except as the rabbit in Winnie the Pooh, behind makeup that hid who she was.

chapters.title: I Threw the Jet Magazine Across the Room
chapters.topic: Emmett Till and Reverend George Lee
chapters.summary: Simpson, about nine when Emmett Till was lynched in 1955, remembers screaming and throwing the Jet magazine across the room at the photograph of his body, the fear that gripped Black children, and the killing of the Reverend George Lee, a friend of her NAACP-member father, who appeared on the cover of a funeral newsletter the family later lost.

chapters.title: No Immediate Impact From Brown
chapters.summary: She reflects that the Brown decision had no immediate impact in her area beyond new separate-but-equal buildings rising without libraries or laboratories, and turns to the church as the center of family and community life, with Sunday services, BTU, and Wednesday Bible study, her father a Sunday school teacher whose lessons her mother drew out on butcher-block paper.

chapters.title: A Beautiful Library and a Culture Shock
chapters.summary: Simpson explains that her aging parents sent her at fourteen to live with her sister in Racine for a solid education, always meaning it as temporary, and describes the culture shock of Washington Park High School, where she interacted with white people for the first time, marveled at the library and athletic equipment, and played field hockey, soccer, and tennis.

chapters.title: Embarrassed to Be From Mississippi
chapters.summary: She recalls being teased and embarrassed about being from Mississippi, called Bigfoot country, a stepsister named Ruth Days who became her protector and best friend, that Racine did not have the segregation she had known, and that the few summer jobs went to white kids, so the only work she could get was babysitting.

chapters.title: I Found Exactly What I Was Looking For
chapters.topic: The Mass Meeting and Her Father's Bargain
chapters.summary: Simpson tells why she came back for her last semester at Amanda Elzy rather than graduate in Wisconsin, attending a mass meeting in Greenwood where freedom songs and young leaders amazed her, naming Hollis Watkins, Lawrence Guyot, John O'Neal, and Mary Lane and meeting her lifelong friend June Johnson, and how her reluctant father agreed to let her join full time only if she named someone in charge, then telephoned Lawrence Guyot himself, so that Guyot told her in the SNCC office her daddy had put him in charge of her.

chapters.title: Selected for Citizenship School
chapters.topic: Annelle Ponder and the Trip South
chapters.summary: Simpson describes coming to know Fannie Lou Hamer when Annelle Ponder of SCLC selected a group, including Hamer and June Johnson, to attend a two-week citizenship school in Charleston, South Carolina, and Ponder telling the group she would use the white waiting rooms on the way because the Interstate Commerce Commission had outlawed separate facilities, leaving it to each of them whether to follow.

chapters.title: What It Meant to Be a Citizen
chapters.summary: She recalls Myles Horton of Highlander, Septima Clark, Esau Jenkins, and Guy and Candie Carawan teaching the basics of citizenship and the right to vote, the duty to return and run workshops registering others, and how she carried that into door-to-door canvassing in Greenwood and her hometown of Itta Bena, where even her own parents, who had never registered, finally did because of her.

chapters.title: Car Loads of Highway Patrolmen
chapters.summary: On the bus trip back from South Carolina, Simpson describes the group using the white waiting rooms without incident until they stopped at Winona, Mississippi, where highway patrolmen and local police grabbed those who got off to use the restroom, piled them into a car as Annelle Ponder wrote down license numbers, and arrested Fannie Lou Hamer when she got off the bus to ask what was happening.

chapters.title: I Still Wasn't Afraid
chapters.summary: She recounts Annelle Ponder, college-educated and soft-spoken, asking why they had been arrested and citing the Interstate Commerce Commission, the jailer ordering her to shut up and striking her when she refused to say sir, and the teenaged Simpson speaking up without fear until the jailer jabbed her with his ring of keys and stamped her foot, with Ponder urging her to say nothing more.

chapters.title: You Are Black Like Me
chapters.summary: Simpson describes the row of cells, how the jailer made two drunken trustees beat the prisoners one by one with a strap, hearing Annelle Ponder moan and return bloody, then June Johnson, who at fourteen looked older, and Fannie Lou Hamer screaming and asking the men why they would beat her when they were Black like her, before Simpson's own turn was cut short after a single blow and she later learned Lawrence Guyot had been arrested and badly beaten nearby.

chapters.title: Walk With Me, Lord
chapters.topic: A Washcloth and a Gospel Song
chapters.summary: She describes nursing the feverish, swollen, bruised Fannie Lou Hamer through the night in their shared cell with cold water on a washcloth, and how, when Hamer's pain eased, the two quietly sang for themselves, Hamer wanting her gospel songs rather than her signature This Little Light of Mine, settling on Walk With Me, which calmed her and which moves Simpson to tears in the telling.

chapters.title: I've Got Some Sad News
chapters.topic: Refusing to Be Moved, and Medgar Evers
chapters.summary: Simpson recounts SNCC's lawyers reaching the jail so the beatings stopped, the group refusing to be moved at night for fear of being taken into the dark to disappear, and being held until around Wednesday when Andrew Young came to free them and first told them the sad news that Medgar Evers had been assassinated the night before, ambushed on his way home from a Jackson meeting, so the joy of release was overshadowed.

chapters.title: A Watershed for Mrs. Hamer
chapters.topic: A Turning Point and the Oxford Trial
chapters.summary: She frames Winona as a watershed for Hamer, after which Hamer came into her own as a national figure who told the story at the next year's Democratic National Convention, and recounts the trial in Oxford where, barred from hearing other testimony, Simpson was asked only whether she was arrested and beaten and answered no because she took just one lash, given no chance to describe what she had heard and seen.

chapters.title: A Freedom Vote to Prove a Point
chapters.summary: Simpson explains the COFO Freedom Vote meant to disprove the claim that Black people did not want to vote, her work running mock voting workshops in Clarksdale and elsewhere drawing on her citizenship-school training, and Aaron Henry, the candidate for governor on the ticket, arranging for her to hold workshops at his church while she stayed at a local woman's home.

chapters.title: Bring Down the Children of Affluent People
chapters.summary: She recounts the national attention paid to the white Yale and Stanford students who helped with the Freedom Vote, the heated SNCC and COFO discussions about bringing hundreds of Northern volunteers into Mississippi so the country would pay attention, the Oxford, Ohio orientation she left early when her mother took sick, and the workshops dramatizing the violence and nonviolent self-protection volunteers might need.

chapters.title: Manning the WATS Line
chapters.topic: The Three Disappear and the COFO Office
chapters.summary: Simpson recalls word arriving at the orientation that James Chaney, Michael Schwerner, and Andrew Goodman had disappeared and were understood to be dead, and her move to Jackson rather than college to staff the COFO office shared by SNCC, CORE, the NAACP, and SCLC, learning to work the WATS line that had to be manned around the clock so volunteers in trouble anywhere in the state could call in and get word out.
related_events: Murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner

chapters.title: Everyone Came Through Jackson
chapters.summary: Simpson tells how everyone passed through the Jackson office that summer, including the man she would marry, Jesse Moore, father of her five children, with Bob Moses and Dave Dennis sharing leadership and friends like Gwen Gillon, Mary King, Casey Hayden, and Dona Richards, and remembers Worth Long, an oral historian who later helped build the Delta Blues Festival, amid the violence of the summer including the McGhee brothers beaten in Greenwood for integrating theaters.

chapters.title: We Rode a Bus and Couldn't Go In
chapters.summary: Simpson explains the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, formed because the state's all-white delegation excluded Black voters, and her own trip by bus to the Atlantic City convention not as an official delegate, where the group spent most of its time outside the convention center, sharing the few badges to let people slip in for a few minutes at a time.

chapters.title: One of Us Is Tired
chapters.summary: She describes hearing of Fannie Lou Hamer's televised testimony and President Johnson calling a press conference to get her off the air, the singing of freedom songs on the boardwalk, and the rift that opened over the offered two-seats-at-large compromise, which Hamer, Annie Devine, and Victoria Gray rejected as unfair while many wanted to accept, leaving Simpson eager to get back to the field.

chapters.title: A Man Who Spoke French
chapters.summary: Simpson recounts deciding in September 1964 to enroll in college on a work-study program alongside Glenda Gillon, Hollis Watkins, MacArthur Cotton, and others, first hearing Tougaloo praised by a French-speaking recruiter at a career day at Amanda Elzy, and only later recalling that her great-grandfather, whom her mother called Professor Jeff Davis Pearson, had himself attended Tougaloo after Reconstruction.

chapters.title: An Oasis for the Movement
chapters.topic: Tougaloo's Reputation and a Hard Transition
chapters.summary: She describes Tougaloo as a private school that was an oasis for the movement, known for turning out lawyers and doctors, which she reached when someone arranged scholarships for about ten movement veterans, and how hard it was for veterans who had been to the March on Washington and met Martin Luther King and Stokely Carmichael to settle into campus life among students chasing sororities, especially after she married early in 1965 and began having children.

chapters.title: Slacks and an Afro in Dr. Townsend's Class
chapters.summary: She recalls the strict Dr. Naomi Townsend, in whose English class girls could not wear pants, calling Simpson out for arriving in slacks and questioning her afro, one of the few on campus in 1964, the little white Honda motorcycle that added to her movement notoriety, and Dr. Ernst Borinski, who taught her German and whose legendary forums brought internationally known visitors to the campus.

chapters.part: A Life of Work and Family
chapters.title: Trying to Go Back to School
chapters.summary: Simpson recounts raising five children while repeatedly returning to school, Jackson State for nursing courses she abandoned, and finally Millsaps College around 1986, a predominantly white liberal arts school she found the most difficult she had attended yet where she did her best work, going full time while working full time and raising children before she graduated and held her own among younger students.

chapters.part: A Life of Work and Family
chapters.title: A Legal Secretary for Civil Rights
chapters.topic: Jobs and Grant Work After the Movement
chapters.summary: She describes her early career, training a year in stenography through an MDTA Great Society program at Hinds Community College to become a legal secretary for the Lawyers Committee for Civil Rights Under Law, then Head Start through Friends of Children of Mississippi, a health clinic in Mendenhall, AmeriCorps programs at Delta State, her marriage to Les, and their work as community coaches for the Kellogg Foundation's Mid-South Delta Initiative across Mississippi, Louisiana, and Arkansas.

chapters.part: A Life of Work and Family
chapters.title: From the Delta to Ghana
chapters.summary: Simpson describes a side proposal that funded trips to Africa for community participants, including a mixed-age group to Ghana and a later one for younger people, among them Mrs. Ayers, the seventy-five-year-old widow of activist Jake Ayers of the Ayers v. Fordice case, and her last work doing social work for about three years helping older people displaced by Hurricane Katrina who had moved to the Jackson area navigate the system for health care, food, and other needs.

chapters.title: You Have to Walk Into the Storm
chapters.summary: Asked about the major changes the movement brought, Simpson names Black people gaining a sense of their own worth and reclaiming power they had given away, invokes a Bernice Reagon song about walking into the storm to reach change, and notes she now lives in a once-off-limits white neighborhood though Jackson's schools, drained by white flight, are nearly all Black again.

chapters.title: The Power Structure Hasn't Changed
chapters.summary: She argues that Black elected officials, including a Black mayor, school board, and city council in Jackson, have not visibly benefited the masses while the economic power structure stayed white-owned and businesses fled to Madison and Rankin counties, eroding the tax base and the schools, and laments the crime and the black-on-black killing that was almost unknown when she was a child, which she ties to drugs and gangs imported from the North.

chapters.title: Earl, Orlando, Omar, T, and Jessica
chapters.summary: In the closing exchange Simpson describes her five children: Earl, who owns a construction business; Orlando, a fireman; Omar, an IT specialist returning to Jackson State for an engineering degree; T, who holds a doctorate and teaches at Ohio State with a book coming; and Jessica, a Michigan-trained attorney, before thanking the interviewer as the session ends.

loc_video.caption: Euvester Simpson oral history interview conducted by John Dittmer in Jackson, Mississippi, 2013 March 12
